materials: Printed sentence with phrase boundaries marked (e.g., with swoops or lines under chunks)
parent_prompt: Run your finger under the first group of words as you read them: 'The little boy'. Pause. Now the next group: 'went to the store'.
success_condition: Child successfully sweeps finger under phrases and pauses at boundaries.
failure_condition: If they stop at every word, hold their finger and guide the smooth sweep.
reasoning_check: How does sweeping your finger help you read the words together?

parent_prompt: I'm going to say a group of words that belong together. You echo them back smoothly. 'in the morning' (wait for echo). 'we ate breakfast' (wait for echo).
success_condition: Child echoes the phrases smoothly without breaking them into choppy words.
failure_condition: If they chop it up, repeat it smoothly and ask them to sing it like a song.
reasoning_check: Did 'in the morning' sound like one big idea or three separate ideas?

materials: Printed passage with phrase boundaries marked (e.g., / or swoops)
parent_prompt: Read this short paragraph out loud. Pause slightly wherever you see the slash mark (/). Try to make the words between the slashes flow together smoothly.
success_condition: Child reads the passage, grouping words according to the provided boundaries.
failure_condition: If they ignore the marks, stop them and model the first sentence again.
reasoning_check: Did pausing at the slashes make the story easier to understand?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Passage pre-marked with slashes for phrasing. E.g., The boy ran / across the field / to catch the ball.]

parent_prompt: Read this sentence silently. Take your pencil and draw a slash (/) where you think a natural pause should go, separating the 'who' from the 'what they did'. Then read it aloud.
success_condition: Child correctly identifies phrase boundaries (e.g., subject/predicate or prepositional phrases) and reads it smoothly.
failure_condition: If they mark every word, remind them we are looking for groups of words that belong together.
reasoning_check: Why did you put the pause right there?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Unmarked sentence requiring the child to determine phrase boundaries.]

parent_prompt: Read this paragraph out loud. Focus on reading in phrases, not word-by-word. Think about how you would say it if you were just talking to me.
success_condition: Child reads the passage with conversational phrasing, abandoning word-by-word reading.
failure_condition: If choppy, have them read the same sentence three times until it smooths out.
reasoning_check: Did it sound more like reading or more like talking?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Passage designed for fluency practice, full of prepositional phrases.]

parent_prompt: Listen to me read this sentence: 'The. Cat. Sat. On. The. Mat.' Did I read that well? What did I do wrong?
success_condition: Child identifies that you read word-by-word (choppy) instead of in phrases.
failure_condition: If they say it was good, ask: 'Is that how we talk?'
reasoning_check: How should I read it instead?

parent_prompt: Listen to me read this: 'The boy walked to / the store to buy / some milk.' Did my pauses sound natural, or did I break up groups of words that belong together?
success_condition: Child identifies that the pauses were unnatural (splitting prepositional phrases or verb phrases awkwardly).
failure_condition: If they think it's fine, read it again with proper phrasing and ask which sounds better.
reasoning_check: Where should the pauses actually go?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Passage read with intentionally awkward phrase breaks.]

parent_prompt: I will read this sentence two ways. Way 1: 'The dog / ran fast / to the house.' Way 2: 'The dog ran / fast to the / house.' Which way made the story easier to understand?

failure_condition: If they pick 2, ask them what 'fast to the' means by itself.

parent_prompt: You are the director! Take this paragraph and draw slashes where I should pause. I will read it exactly how you mark it.

parent_prompt: Here is a story you haven't seen before. Read it out loud to me. Do your best to make it sound smooth and interesting, not choppy.

parent_prompt: Listen to me read this sentence. (Read very fast). Now listen again. (Read very slow). Which way was easier to understand and why?
success_condition: Child identifies that neither extreme is good; a middle pace is best.
failure_condition: If they prefer the fast one, ask them to repeat what you just said.
reasoning_check: Why is reading too fast a problem?

parent_prompt: I am going to read this passage at a 'just right' walking pace. Try to point to the words exactly as I say them, not too fast, not too slow.
success_condition: Child tracks the text accurately at a fluent conversational pace.
failure_condition: If they lag behind or speed ahead, guide their finger to match your voice.
reasoning_check: Did we move our fingers like a race car, or like someone taking a walk?

parent_prompt: If I read so fast that I skip words and get out of breath, am I reading at a good rate? What if I read so slow I forget what the beginning of the sentence was about?
success_condition: Child articulates that both extremes hinder comprehension.
failure_condition: If they don't know, explain the concept of a 'talking speed'.
reasoning_check: What speed should we aim for when reading out loud?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Discussion on reading speed.]

parent_prompt: Read this paragraph out loud. I will time you. Don't rush and make mistakes, just read smoothly. Ready? Go.
success_condition: Child reads the passage. Note the time. (Goal is age-appropriate WPM, usually 60-90 WPM in Band 2).
failure_condition: If they read too fast and make many errors, say: 'This isn't a race. Accuracy is more important than speed.'
reasoning_check: Did you feel rushed, or did it feel like a comfortable speed?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Paragraph suitable for WPM calculation.]

parent_prompt: You read that paragraph in 45 seconds last time. Let's read it again. Try to beat your time, BUT if you make a mistake, you have to go back and fix it.
success_condition: Child reads the passage slightly faster due to familiarity, maintaining accuracy.
failure_condition: If they speed read and ignore errors, stop the timer. 'We have to read it right first.'
reasoning_check: Why was it easier to read it faster the second time?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Same paragraph as previous task.]

parent_prompt: I will tap my finger on the table in a steady beat. Try to read the words to match my steady beat. Don't speed up!
success_condition: Child maintains a steady reading rate without rushing or dragging.
failure_condition: If they ignore the beat, stop and have them just say 'da da da' to the beat first.
reasoning_check: How does keeping a steady beat help your reading sound better?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Passage read to a steady physical beat.]

parent_prompt: Listen to me read this paragraph. (Read it slightly too fast, slurring some words). Give me advice: Should I speed up, slow down, or keep the same speed?
success_condition: Child identifies you are reading too fast and advises slowing down.
failure_condition: If they say it was good, ask: 'Could you understand every word I said?'
reasoning_check: Why is reading that fast a bad idea?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Passage intentionally read too fast.]

parent_prompt: If a student reads 100 words in a minute but gets 20 of them wrong, and another student reads 60 words in a minute and gets all of them right, who is the better reader?
success_condition: Child identifies the slower, accurate reader as better.
failure_condition: If they pick the fast one, explain that reading is about understanding, not just speed.
reasoning_check: Why doesn't speed matter if you get the words wrong?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Discussion based on hypothetical readers.]

parent_prompt: Read this sentence. It has commas and a full stop. When you read fast, you might ignore them. Read it at a good rate, and make sure I hear you pause at the punctuation.
success_condition: Child reads at a controlled rate, observing punctuation boundaries.
failure_condition: If they barrel through commas, have them read it again and clap at every comma.
reasoning_check: How does punctuation control how fast we read?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Sentence heavily reliant on punctuation for meaning and pacing.]

materials: A printed poem or rhythmic text
parent_prompt: You are in charge of how fast we read this poem together. Read it out loud, and I will try to match your exact speed. Make sure you pick a good 'talking speed.'
success_condition: Child sets and maintains an appropriate reading rate while reading chorally with the parent.
failure_condition: If they read too fast, the parent should struggle to keep up intentionally to demonstrate.
reasoning_check: Was it hard to keep the same speed the whole time?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [A short poem suitable for choral reading.]

materials: Two short printed sentences (one urgent, one sleepy)
parent_prompt: Look at sentence A: 'Quick, the bus is leaving!' Look at sentence B: 'The old turtle slowly went to sleep.' Read them out loud. Should you read them at the exact same speed?
success_condition: Child reads sentence A faster and sentence B slower, adjusting rate for meaning.
failure_condition: If they read both identically, ask them what is happening in each sentence.
reasoning_check: Why did you change your speed for the second sentence?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Two sentences demanding different reading rates based on content.]

parent_prompt: Read this paragraph. Time yourself. When you finish, tell me: Did I read too fast, too slow, or just right? Did I understand what I read?
success_condition: Child reads, self-monitors their rate, and evaluates their comprehension.
failure_condition: If their self-evaluation doesn't match reality (e.g., they rushed but say it was perfect), provide gentle feedback.
reasoning_check: How do you know if you are reading at the right speed?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Passage suitable for self-timed reading.]

task_type: Reading a Story to an Audience
materials: A familiar storybook or printed story
parent_prompt: I want you to read this story to your younger sibling (or a stuffed animal/pet). Read it at a speed that helps them understand and enjoy the story.
success_condition: Child demonstrates appropriate reading rate and fluency in an authentic read-aloud context.
failure_condition: Do not correct during the reading. Evaluate if the rate facilitated comprehension for the audience.
reasoning_check: Did you have to slow down so they could understand the story better?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Requires a familiar physical book.]

parent_prompt: Listen to me say this: 'I can't believe we are going to the zoo!' Did my voice go up or down at the end? Did I sound bored or excited?
success_condition: Child identifies the pitch going up and the excited tone.
failure_condition: If they say bored, say it again completely flat, then contrast with the excited version.
reasoning_check: How does my voice change when I'm excited?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Exclamatory sentence read with high expression.]

parent_prompt: When I read a sentence that ends in a question mark (?), shrug your shoulders. When it ends in an exclamation mark (!), throw your hands up. 'Where is the dog?' (wait). 'There he is!' (wait).
success_condition: Child physically responds to the auditory cues of different sentence types.
failure_condition: If they miss the cue, explain that our voice goes up for a question and gets loud/strong for an exclamation.
reasoning_check: How does your voice tell someone there is a question mark at the end of the sentence?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Interrogative and exclamatory sentences read aloud.]

parent_prompt: I'm going to say a sentence in a grumpy voice. You echo it back in the exact same grumpy voice. 'I don't want to eat my vegetables.'
success_condition: Child accurately mimics the tone and inflection.
failure_condition: If they say it normally, exaggerate the grumpiness and ask them to act like an actor.
reasoning_check: How did making your voice grumpy change the meaning of the words?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Sentence to be echoed with specific emotional inflection.]

materials: Printed sentences with varied end punctuation
parent_prompt: Read these three sentences out loud. Make sure your voice tells me which one has a full stop, which has a question mark, and which has an exclamation mark.
success_condition: Child reads the sentences with appropriate inflection (flat, rising pitch, strong emphasis).
failure_condition: If they read them all the same, model the difference and have them repeat.
reasoning_check: How did your voice change for the question?

default.sentences: The cat is here. Is the cat here? The cat is here!

ug.sentences: The cat is here. Is the cat here? The cat is here!

text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Three identical sentences ending in ., ?, and ! respectively.]

materials: Printed sentence with one word bolded
parent_prompt: Read this sentence, but make your voice extra strong on the bold word: 'I did **not** eat the last cookie.' Now read it again emphasizing a different word: '**I** did not eat the last cookie.'
success_condition: Child successfully shifts emphasis to change the nuance of the sentence.
failure_condition: If they struggle, say it for them and ask them to copy.
reasoning_check: How does changing the strong word change the meaning of the sentence?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Sentence designed to be read with shifting word emphasis.]

parent_prompt: Read this story where a big bear is talking to a tiny mouse. Use a deep voice for the bear, and a squeaky voice for the mouse.
success_condition: Child distinguishes speakers by altering pitch and tone.
failure_condition: If they use their normal voice, remind them: 'You are an actor right now. Be the bear!'
reasoning_check: How do character voices make a story more fun to listen to?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Short dialogue between a large character and a small character.]

parent_prompt: Listen to me read this scary part of a story. (Read it in a very happy, cheerful voice). Did my voice match what was happening in the story?
success_condition: Child identifies the mismatch between content and tone.
failure_condition: If they say it was fine, ask: 'If something scary is happening, do people sound happy?'
reasoning_check: How should I have used my voice instead?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [A suspenseful passage read intentionally with inappropriate cheerful prosody.]

parent_prompt: I'm going to read this question: 'Where are my shoes.' (Read it with a flat, falling pitch like a statement). What did I forget to do with my voice at the end?
success_condition: Child identifies that the pitch should rise at the end of a question.
failure_condition: If they don't know, model the correct rising pitch.
reasoning_check: Can you read it the right way?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [A question read with statement intonation.]

parent_prompt: Look at this sentence: 'That is a huge dog!' Which word should we say the loudest to make it sound right? Huge or dog?
success_condition: Child selects 'huge' to emphasize the descriptive element.
failure_condition: If they select 'dog', read it both ways and ask which sounds more natural.
reasoning_check: Why does emphasizing 'huge' make the sentence better?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Exclamatory sentence with a clear focal adjective.]

parent_prompt: Read this paragraph silently. Think about how it should sound. Underline two words that you think should be read LOUDLY or with strong feeling. Then read it out loud to me.
success_condition: Child independently selects words for emphasis and executes the reading accordingly.
failure_condition: If they underline random words like 'the', ask: 'Does emphasizing *the* change the meaning?'
reasoning_check: Why did you choose to make those specific words strong?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Paragraph with clear opportunities for expressive emphasis.]

parent_prompt: Write a short sentence of dialogue where someone is whispering because they are hiding. Then write a sentence where they are shouting because they are angry.
success_condition: Child composes sentences that implicitly demand specific prosodic delivery.
failure_condition: If the content doesn't match the emotion, prompt them to think about what people say when hiding/angry.
reasoning_check: Read your sentences out loud to me using the right voices.
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Not required for independent writing.]

parent_prompt: Explain to me why punctuation marks are like road signs for our voice when we read out loud. Give an example.
success_condition: Child articulates that punctuation dictates stops, pauses, and pitch changes.
failure_condition: If they struggle, ask: 'What happens if we ignore a stop sign on the road? What if we ignore a full stop in a book?'
reasoning_check: How does a question mark tell your voice what to do?

materials: A printed script or dialogue-heavy passage
parent_prompt: We are going to do a radio play! You read this part, and I'll read this part. You have to use your voice to show exactly how your character feels.
success_condition: Child reads their part fluently, integrating appropriate phrasing, rate, and expressive prosody.
failure_condition: Do not correct during the performance. If flat and robotic, review Execute level tasks.
reasoning_check: Did you feel like you were acting with your voice?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [A short, emotionally varied dialogue script between two characters.]

parent_prompt: Listen to this short poem: 'The fat cat / sat on the mat.' Which words rhyme? What sound do they all have at the end?
success_condition: Child identifies cat, sat, mat and the /at/ sound.
failure_condition: If they don't hear it, emphasize the ends of the words: c-AT, s-AT.
reasoning_check: Why do poems often use rhyming words?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Simple rhyming couplet.]

parent_prompt: I am going to say a rhyme. I want you to clap on the strong beats with me. 'Hickory dickory dock, the mouse ran up the clock.'
success_condition: Child claps along with the metrical rhythm of the text.
failure_condition: If they clap on every syllable, guide their hands to only clap the stressed beats.
reasoning_check: Did that sound like a regular story, or more like a song?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [A highly rhythmic, metered nursery rhyme or chant.]

materials: A printed poem and a printed paragraph
parent_prompt: Look at this poem, and look at this regular story paragraph. How do they look different on the page? Look at where the lines end.
success_condition: Child identifies that poems have short lines and don't go all the way to the edge of the page.
failure_condition: If they don't see it, trace the ragged right edge of the poem vs the blocky paragraph.
reasoning_check: Why do you think the poet chopped the lines up like that?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [Visually contrast a stanza of poetry with a prose paragraph.]

materials: Printed poem missing the last word
parent_prompt: Read this poem. The very last word is missing. Think of a word that makes sense AND rhymes with the line above it. Tell it to me, then write it in the blank.
success_condition: Child orally provides and writes a valid rhyming word that fits the context.
failure_condition: If it doesn't rhyme, ask: 'What word does it need to sound like?' If it doesn't make sense, ask: 'Does that fit the story?'
reasoning_check: How did you know that was the right word to use?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [A 4-line AABB poem missing the final B rhyme word.]

failure_condition: If the lines don't make sense together, prompt them to connect the ideas.

parent_prompt: Read this poem out loud. Pay attention to the line breaks. Pause slightly at the end of each line, even if there isn't a full stop, so we can hear the rhythm.
success_condition: Child reads the poem, honoring line breaks and emphasizing the meter.
failure_condition: If they read it like prose (running lines together), model the rhythmic pauses and have them try again.
reasoning_check: How does pausing at the end of the line help the poem sound better?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [A 4-line poem requiring pausing at line breaks to maintain meter.]

parent_prompt: Listen to this: 'Roses are red, violets are blue, sugar is sweet, and I like orange.' Does that sound like a good poem? What went wrong at the end?
success_condition: Child identifies that the rhyming pattern was broken.
failure_condition: If they don't hear it, ask: 'Does orange rhyme with blue?'
reasoning_check: What word could we use instead to fix the rhyme?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [A standard poem with the final rhyme intentionally broken.]

parent_prompt: Listen: 'Twinkle twinkle little star, how I wonder what you are up in the sky so very high.' Did I read that with the right beat, or did I mess up the rhythm?
success_condition: Child identifies that running the lines together ruined the song's meter/rhythm.
failure_condition: If they think it's fine, sing it correctly and ask them to compare.
reasoning_check: Why does rhythm matter in a poem or song?
text_passage: PLACEHOLDER: [A highly rhythmic poem read without regard for meter.]

parent_prompt: Poems often use words that paint a picture in your mind. Look at this poem. Find one phrase that helps you 'see' or 'hear' what the poet is talking about.
success_condition: Child locates descriptive, sensory language within the poetic text.
failure_condition: If they struggle, ask: 'What words tell us what color it is? Or how loud it is?'
reasoning_check: How do those words help make the poem interesting?

parent_prompt: Write your name downwards on the paper. Now write a describing word or short phrase that starts with each letter of your name. This is an acrostic poem!

failure_condition: If they struggle to find words, help them brainstorm traits or things they like.
reasoning_check: Does an acrostic poem have to rhyme? (No).

success_condition: Child creates a rhythmic, functional chant and demonstrates it with steady beat.
failure_condition: Do not correct during the task. The goal is applying rhythm to language creatively.
reasoning_check: Does your chant match the rhythm of the work we are doing?
